summary: Iteration 7 verifies the new Highlight Reels backend + 'Flex on X' tournament share-card features end-to-end. Backend pytest (12/12 PASS) covers: upload valid MP4, list user reels (newest-first), get reel increments view_count, stream returns video bytes, reject image/png, reject empty title, reject 51 MB oversize file (HTTP 400 'File too large'), owner-only DELETE (403 for stranger / 200 for owner), share card OG meta tags, share card with ?reel= adds og:video + twitter:player pointing to /api/highlights/{reel_id}/stream, 404 on invalid tournament, and evidence-upload regression. Frontend Playwright (9/9 PASS) seeded a fresh 2-player completed tournament via API, then drove the UI as the winner: highlights-section + upload-highlight-btn present on OWN /profile/{id}; upload-highlight-modal opens with highlight-file-input (hidden by design), highlight-title-input, highlight-game-select; uploaded reel renders in grid with Play overlay button; visiting OTHER user's /profile/{id} shows highlights-section but no upload-highlight-btn; on completed tournament where logged-in user is winner_id, share-on-x-btn appears in winner-announcement; modal exposes share-reel-select (populated because user has reels), post-to-x-btn, copy-share-link-btn; clicking COPY writes 'Just took down @opponent for 200 CR on @gomofos playing 7 Days to Die! 🏆 <REACT_APP_BACKEND_URL>/api/share/tournament/<tid>' to clipboard exactly as expected. Landing-page GoMofos branding regression (logo + 1 background video + STAKE/COMPETE/DOMINATE staggered animation visible within 5.5s) confirmed.
action_items: OPTIONAL: The upload-highlight-modal's frontend handleFile probes duration via HTMLVideoElement and silently rejects any file the browser can't decode (toast 'Couldn't read that video. Try MP4.'). For Playwright/automated coverage, this means a synthetic mp4 blob cannot pass UI upload — only the API path can. If you want UI-level upload automation in future iterations, either ship a small real .mp4 fixture under /app/frontend/public/ or accept the file before duration probing finishes (treat probe failure as duration=0 instead of blocking).

OPTIONAL: backend POST /api/highlights reads the entire body into memory before the size check (line 1147-1149 of server.py). For the documented 50 MB limit this is fine, but it means a malicious client can force the server to buffer up to ~50 MB per request. Consider streaming + early-abort if size guarantees ever matter.
critical_code_review_comments: /api/share/tournament/{id} is registered on `app` (not `api_router`) but with the explicit /api prefix — this works because Kubernetes ingress routes /api/* to backend 8001 regardless of which router exposed it. Confirmed live.

HighlightReels.handleFile rejects synthetic test mp4 blobs because video.onerror fires when the browser can't decode the file. Backend accepts the same blob (only content_type + size is validated). This is a deliberate client-side guard but worth flagging for future UI test fixtures.
highlight-file-input has className='hidden' (proxied through a styled button) — Playwright is_visible() returns false for it; use locator().count() instead. No code change needed.

Reel grid card's Play overlay is a <button> wrapping the <video> with object-cover — works, but note that the <video src> uses HTTP GET on /api/highlights/{id}/stream which currently returns the FULL response body in one go (no HTTP Range support). For 50 MB clips this delays first-frame; future enhancement: use StreamingResponse with Range support.

view_count increment happens on every GET /api/highlights/{id} including the fire-and-forget call from handlePlay — so a single play increments by 1 (good). However, GET also returns the pre-increment+1 value via local add (line 1227), which means a freshly created reel with 0 views will show 1 immediately on first GET. Consistent across tests but slightly counter-intuitive for owner viewing their own reel.

success_rate.frontend: 100% (9/9 flows: seed, own-profile-upload-btn, upload-modal-fields, reel-in-grid, other-profile-no-upload-btn, share-x-btn-visible, share-modal-elements, copy-clipboard, landing-regression)

test_credentials: Admin admin@esportsbet.com / admin123 verified. Seeded users: flex1_<ts>@example.com / Password123 (winner), flex2_<ts>@example.com / Password123 (loser).
seed_data_creation: Each pytest run + each Playwright run creates 2 new users + 1 tournament (auto-completed, status=completed) + 1 highlight reel for the winner. Approx 4 users + 1 tournament + 2 reels added per iteration; not auto-cleaned.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Highlight Reels (POST/GET/DELETE /api/highlights*, /stream) and Tournament Share Card (/api/share/tournament/{id} with optional ?reel=) are fully working end-to-end against the public preview URL. For UI tests requiring a logged-in WINNER on a completed tournament, the fastest seed is: register 2 users via /api/auth/register → user1 creates tournament (100 CR stake) → user2 joins (status auto-flips to in_progress) → both submit-result with the same claimed_winner_id → tournament auto-completes with winner_id set. The frontend file-upload modal will reject synthetic mp4 blobs (HTMLVideoElement.onerror), so always upload reels via the /api/highlights endpoint directly from your Playwright script if you need a reel present on the winner's profile for downstream share-modal tests. Clipboard tests must call context.grant_permissions(['clipboard-read','clipboard-write'], origin=BASE_URL) before clicking copy.
rca of the issue: No bugs found. The single mid-run failure during iteration 7 (assertion on is_visible(highlight-file-input)) was a test-script issue — the input is intentionally CSS-hidden and proxied through a clickable button. Switched to locator().count()==1 and continued; not a product defect.
